concept: Roadside Americana Artist - Comic Relief Living Masterpiece
appearance: Rembrandt Jones appears perpetually suspended between decades, a man who looks fully adult but never old — somewhere between thirty and fifty, depending on the light and the observer. He carries himself with relaxed confidence, the posture of someone accustomed to attention without ever seeming to demand it. His features are handsome in a classic way: strong jaw, expressive eyes, an easy smile that arrives quickly and lingers just long enough to feel intentional. His grooming is impeccable but subtly dated, as though his sense of style locked in decades ago and never felt the need to update. When engaged in conversation, he is warm, charming, and articulate — but when something catches his interest, his focus sharpens unnervingly, his gaze lingering on details others overlook, as if the rest of the world briefly ceases to exist. Rembrandt Jones looks like a man who should be holding court in a smoky lounge rather than standing beneath fluorescent lights — yet somehow belongs equally in both places. His clothing is tailored and expensive, favoring classic cuts: silk shirts, fitted jackets, polished shoes, and colors that nearly clash but never quite do. Everything he wears suggests intention, though the intention itself feels borrowed from another time. His hair is carefully styled in a way that reads as timeless to some and outdated to others, reinforcing the sense that he has stepped sideways through history rather than forward. His expressions are open and genuine, his smile disarming, but when he speaks about art — or something he finds beautiful — his eyes fixate with unsettling intensity. In those moments, it becomes clear that his charm is not an act; it is simply the surface of something far more focused, obsessive, and sincere beneath.
biography: Dealer at The Sands casino in late 1950s/early 1960s Las Vegas. Obsessively attended Rat Pack shows 3+ times per week for years, even after his Embrace. Embraced during Rat Pack era (late 50s/early 60s) by a Las Vegas Toreador who created gaudy casino strip art and saw potential in Rembrandt's obsessive dedication to beauty. Has been a vampire for 30-35 years. Sire subtly encouraged him to leave Vegas and move to Phoenix (paid him, made it appealing) because while he saw potential, Rembrandt became something... unexpected. Now lives in small bungalow west of Camelback Mountain. Accidentally stumbled into Roadside Americana/kitschy nostalgia subculture where his work is actually appreciated, but remains oblivious to this success while desperately trying to impress mainstream Toreador scene and the Primogen. His failure at being a 'proper' Toreador is so complete and earnest that it has become its own form of performance art - a living masterpiece he will never understand. Sire sends him short poems about art every few months, trying to guide him; Rembrandt misinterprets them completely and uses them as inspiration for his next terrible piece.
equipment: Professional camera equipment, thousands of photographs of mundane objects (technically brilliant, questionable subjects), LED sculpture tools and components, vintage roadside Americana collection, velvet paintings, commemorative plates, lava lamps, neon beer signs, 3D holographic pictures, shag carpeting samples, security system equipment

backgroundDetails.Contacts: Deep connections in Roadside Americana/kitschy nostalgia subculture: Muffler Men collectors, vintage motel sign preservationists, Route 66 nostalgists, Googie architecture enthusiasts, vintage Vegas memorabilia dealers, neon sign restoration communities, photographers of abandoned roadside attractions. He doesn't fully realize this is where he's successful.
backgroundDetails.Herd: 2-3 regular people who let him feed. They KNOW what's happening and are into it (possibly from his roadside Americana community). He thinks he's being smooth and mysterious with elaborate 'seduction' techniques. They go along with it because they find him endearing.
backgroundDetails.Resources: Quite wealthy from sire's trust fund/investments plus surprisingly successful sales of his terrible art to roadside Americana collectors. Small bungalow in old neighborhood west of Camelback Mountain filled with his collection.
backgroundDetails.Status: Marginal within Toreador circles. Comic relief to most. Some pretentious theorists write papers about him. Trying desperately to impress the Toreador Primogen.

merits_flaws.name: Luck
merits_flaws.description: Exceptionally lucky - explains how Clumsy x2 doesn't kill him. Things go wrong but work out somehow. The universe keeps him alive because he's accidentally art.
